input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle that has been retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology. Its hardware is organized into lower‑ and upper‑level chassis sections together with dedicated autonomy elements. The vehicle operates as a resource‑constrained platform for adaptive cruise control (ACC) experiments, making it suitable for off‑loading computation to a digital twin because the on‑board electronic control units (ECUs) cannot handle the full ACC workload.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a graphical user interface (UI) displays real‑time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks, and allows users to view, save, and generate reports of the data. (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, desired‑speed calculation, and speed command generation) is executed in the cloud DT; the computed desired speed is sent back to the vehicle for actuation. (3) Remote Management – through the same UI operators can enable or disable ACC, set a target speed, and issue an emergency‑brake command. All services rely on low end‑to‑end latency communication with the vehicle and respect the safety‑critical nature of the system.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation relies on a cloud‑based compute platform to host the off‑loaded ACC algorithm, a low‑latency bi‑directional communication link between the vehicle and the DT, and a customized UI that integrates data visualization, reporting, and command‑and‑control functions. The DT system aggregates the DT Entity, physical and middleware components into a cohesive Digital Twin System (DTS) following community best‑practice architectures.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires clock synchronization between devices with an accuracy of 100 ms, which is the maximum tolerated offset given hardware limitations; this synchronization is needed for computation offloading and safety‑critical vehicular functions.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The digital twin follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture, organizing functionality into three entities (Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity). Each functional entity (FE) is implemented as an individual ROS 2 package containing one or more ROS 2 nodes, and the packages are deployed on separate hardware components, providing a distributed, entity‑based orchestration of data management, control, actuation, and user interaction.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin Entity includes computational models implemented as ROS2 nodes within individual ROS2 packages. One explicit model is a point‑cloud clustering and tracking algorithm that processes geometric data from vehicle sensors. Data sources are the vehicle’s onboard sensors (e.g., LiDAR) accessed through the Physical Twin Entity. Model data and intermediate results are stored by a dedicated Data Storage component. All models and data are orchestrated via ROS2 communication, following the ISO 23247 functional‑entity mapping.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation functionality is encapsulated in the Physical Twin Entity. Actuators are accessed through ROS2 nodes that implement the actuation functional entity, allowing the Digital Twin to issue remote control commands to vehicle effectors (e.g., steering, throttle). The control interface follows the ISO 23247 Data Collection and Device Control Entity pattern, but the documents do not specify actuator types, ranges, or safety constraints.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing hardware is part of the Physical Twin Entity and is interfaced through ROS2 nodes that perform data pre‑processing before forwarding to the Digital Twin Entity. The documents mention generic sensors that provide point‑cloud data for the clustering model, but no specific sensor types, sampling rates, accuracy, or transmission specifications are given.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Raw sensor streams (LiDAR point cloud, motor status, steering servo position, vehicle state) are emitted by the physical twin and transmitted to the digital twin over a Wi‑Fi link using UDP at the transport layer. Upon reception the data enter the ROS2 middleware where they are routed to the Data Pre‑processing functional element (Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, Data Monitoring FE). The Data Translation FE serialises the streams, aligns message types to the ISO 23247 data model and performs unit conversions before they are stored or visualised. Pre‑processing includes clustering and tracking of the LiDAR point cloud on a 2‑D plane to produce object tracks used by the 3‑D visualization. Each new sensor frame triggers the pipeline; no explicit sampling frequency is given in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the digital twin – ACC enable/disable, desired speed set‑point, and emergency‑brake request – are packaged as control messages and sent back to the vehicle over the same Wi‑Fi UDP link. The messages are consumed by the Actuation FE (Motor, Steering, Servo) which executes the actuation on the physical twin. The DT UI allows the operator to issue these commands; the system validates the command type against the ACC service model before transmission. Execution confirmation is provided by the vehicle‑state feedback that is fed back through the same data‑monitoring pipeline.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DTS uses a two‑level network architecture. Inside the digital twin, all software components communicate via ROS 2 middleware (publish/subscribe). Interaction between the physical twin and the digital twin crosses the physical boundary over a Wi‑Fi network, using UDP as the transport protocol. The architecture therefore comprises a ROS 2 intra‑entity mesh and an inter‑entity Wi‑Fi/UDP link. The design requires low end‑to‑end latency to satisfy safety‑critical ACC control, though exact latency or bandwidth figures are not specified. Reliability is addressed by ROS 2 quality‑of‑service settings and by the stateless nature of UDP; no explicit redundancy or security mechanisms are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The digital twin services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) are intended to run on external compute resources that are more capable than the vehicle’s ECU, exemplified by cloud‑based execution of the ACC algorithm. The DTS therefore can be deployed on a cloud platform (or edge server) that provides sufficient CPU for the ACC decision logic, storage for collected sensor data and generated reports, and network connectivity to the vehicle. Scalability is achieved by allocating additional cloud instances for larger data volumes, though concrete scaling mechanisms are not detailed. Availability is implied by the need for continuous remote management, but specific redundancy or SLA provisions are not mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture. Key software components are identified and mapped onto Functional Entities (FEs). Each FE is realized as an individual ROS 2 package containing one or more ROS 2 nodes that implement the required functionality. The mapping organises components into the User Entity (User Interface FE split into 3‑D Visualization and Control Panel), the Digital Twin Entity (data‑management and control mechanisms) and the Physical Twin Entity (data pre‑processing, actuation, device communication such as point‑cloud clustering & tracking). Development deviates from the standard by introducing a dedicated Physical Twin Entity that hosts compute, sensors and actuators. The process description does not include explicit testing, version‑control, or quality‑assurance practices.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system explicitly adheres to ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing (all four parts). It adopts the entity‑based reference architecture comprising Observable Manufacturing Elements, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. The implementation may employ OPC Unified Architecture for device coordination, as suggested by the standard. Clock‑synchronisation requirements from vehicular safety‑critical standards (sub‑100 ms) are recognised, and the implementation targets 100 ms accuracy due to hardware constraints. No additional certification or interoperability frameworks are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The case study notes that the ISO 23247 reference architecture (RA) is inadequate for safety‑critical automotive Digital Twin applications. It recommends elevating safety to a first‑class citizen by explicitly adopting existing automotive functional‑safety standards, specifically ISO 26262. This implies that safety analyses required by ISO 26262—such as hazard analysis, risk assessment, and definition of safety goals—should be integrated into the DT development process. The document does not detail specific cybersecurity protocols, access‑control or authentication mechanisms, but emphasizes that safety considerations must be addressed through alignment with ISO 26262's systematic safety lifecycle.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.

functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.

sensor and control technology

chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle

ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation

data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while

same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations
